source_reference: Ajmani et al., 'yoU aRe a Liar', SecWeb 2022, hostname confusion (backslash before @); bytes verified vs wspr-ncsu/urlparsing-framework (BSD-3-Clause)
notes: Section V.1: backslash in authority. WHATWG maps \ to / for special schemes -> google.com is host; RFC treats google.com:80\ as userinfo -> yahoo.com is host. rurl reproduces BOTH via profiles (whatwg=google.com, rfc=yahoo.com).

source_reference: Ajmani et al., 'yoU aRe a Liar', SecWeb 2022, control-character confusion (literal TAB 0x09 in host); bytes verified vs wspr-ncsu/urlparsing-framework (BSD-3-Clause)
paper_claimed_behavior: chrome/url-parse host=xdavidhu.me<TAB>est; urllib3/uri-js host=xdavidhu.me%09est; urllib/whatwg-url host=xdavidhu.meest; php host=xdavidhu.me_est; curl Error
notes: Section V.3: WHATWG strips ASCII tab/newline before parsing -> accepts. rurl rejects a control char in the authority (does not silently strip). NOTE: the paper displays this input as `xdavidhu.me\test...` where `\t` is escape-notation for the TAB byte (parallel to the `\r\n` display in yal-003); the literal bytes are `me`+TAB+`est`, confirmed by every parser column showing `est` (urllib3=me%09est, whatwg-url=meest, php=me_est).

source_reference: Ajmani et al., 'yoU aRe a Liar', SecWeb 2022, control-character confusion (literal CR LF in host; CRLF/SSRF-redis); bytes verified vs wspr-ncsu/urlparsing-framework (BSD-3-Clause)
paper_claimed_behavior: chrome/url-parse host=127.0.0.<CRLF>1; urllib3/uri-js host=127.0.0.%0D%0A1; urllib/whatwg-url host=127.0.0.1; php host=127.0.0._1; curl Error
notes: Section V.3. NOTE: CR (0x0D) does not survive CSV round-trip (normalizes to LF); input_json holds the authoritative CR LF byte sequence. rurl rejects both forms identically (control char in authority).

source_reference: Ajmani et al., 'yoU aRe a Liar', SecWeb 2022, backslash confusion (mixed //\ before host); bytes verified vs wspr-ncsu/urlparsing-framework (BSD-3-Clause)
paper_claimed_behavior: chrome/whatwg-url host=github.com; urllib3/uri-js path=/%5C/%5C/%5Cgithub.com/foo/bar; urllib path=/\/\/\github.com/foo/bar; php path=/\/\/\github.com/foo/bar; curl host=\
notes: Section V.4: WHATWG treats \ as / for special schemes -> collapses to host github.com. rurl whatwg does the same (flags invalid-reverse-solidus); rfc rejects backslash.

source_reference: Ajmani et al., 'yoU aRe a Liar', SecWeb 2022, encoding confusion (non-ASCII host, IDNA); bytes verified vs wspr-ncsu/urlparsing-framework (BSD-3-Clause)
paper_claimed_behavior: chrome/url-parse host=<unicode>; urllib3/uri-js/whatwg-url host=xn--pdk.abc.xn--idk; urllib/php/curl host=<raw unicode>
notes: Section V.7. boundary-by-design (ADR 0002): rurl keeps the host reversibly Unicode; the Punycode/ASCII form is a separate presentation phase (domain_ascii / host_encoding=idna), NOT a parse failure. Same family as Ada `Yagiz.com`.

source_reference: Ajmani et al., 'yoU aRe a Liar', SecWeb 2022, slash confusion (empty authority, special scheme); bytes verified vs wspr-ncsu/urlparsing-framework (BSD-3-Clause)
paper_claimed_behavior: Section VI.B PoC: RFC-empty-host validators pass the check while curl (ignores extra slash) fetches evil.com -> SSRF filter bypass
notes: Section VI.B: browsers/whatwg-url collapse the leading slash -> host evil.com. rurl whatwg lands on evil.com (the fetched/dangerous host).

source_reference: Ajmani et al., 'yoU aRe a Liar', SecWeb 2022, backslash/hostname confusion (validation vs fetch split); bytes verified vs wspr-ncsu/urlparsing-framework (BSD-3-Clause)
paper_claimed_behavior: Section VI.A PoC: urllib3 validates host=example.com (allow-list pass) while urllib fetches host=localhost -> allow-list bypass
notes: Section VI.A: WHATWG maps \ to / -> host example.com; RFC treats example.com:80\ as userinfo -> host localhost. rurl reproduces BOTH via profiles (whatwg=example.com, rfc=localhost).

source_reference: Ajmani et al., 'yoU aRe a Liar', SecWeb 2022, slash confusion (many slashes, non-special scheme); bytes verified vs wspr-ncsu/urlparsing-framework (BSD-3-Clause)
notes: Section V.5. boundary-by-design (ADR 0004): `foo` is outside rurl's closed scheme set (http/https/ftp(s)) -> rurl rejects. WHATWG accepts as a non-special-scheme opaque path.

source_reference: Ajmani et al., 'yoU aRe a Liar', SecWeb 2022, scheme confusion (dotted token before colon); bytes verified vs wspr-ncsu/urlparsing-framework (BSD-3-Clause)
notes: Section V.2. needs-investigation: dots are legal in a scheme, so WHATWG parses scheme=www.php.net (host empty). rurl instead infers http and reads www.php.net as the host (the usability group's behavior).

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Pitfall 1 (Null Bytes), Table 3 U1 (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
notes: NUL (0x00) in UserInfo. NON-RUNNABLE: R strings cannot hold NUL. input_json uses the paper's [0xNN] octet notation.

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Pitfall 2 (Backslash Correction), Table 3 U2 (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
notes: Backslash in UserInfo. WHATWG/browsers correct \ to / -> n.pr is host (Option B); RFC parsers keep n.pr\ as userinfo -> e.gg (Option A). rurl reproduces both: whatwg=n.pr, rfc=e.gg.

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Pitfall 4 (IPv6+ Address Syntax), Table 3 U3 (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
notes: Balanced-but-not-matching brackets `][` in host. WHATWG reference rejects; rurl rejects too.

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Pitfall 7 (Illegal Extra Delimiters), Table 3 U4 (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
notes: `#` then `@` (duplicate delimiter). # starts the fragment -> n.pr is host (Option A); @e.gg lands in the fragment.

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Pitfall 3 (Overeager Percent Decoding), Table 3 U5 (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
notes: %2e decodes to '.' -> host n.pr.e.gg (Option A). rurl decodes in both profiles.

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Pitfall 6 (Low ASCII Bytes), Table 3 U6 (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
notes: LF (0x0A) in host. WHATWG strips ASCII newline -> host n.pre.gg (Option A); rurl rejects the control char (same family as yoU-aRe-a-Liar yal-002/003).

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Pitfall 5 (Automatic Punycode Conversion), Table 3 U7 (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
notes: Raw octets DD 9A DC BD in host. NON-RUNNABLE: not valid UTF-8, not storable/round-trippable. input_json uses the paper's [0xNN] octet notation. standard_expectation=failure: the NodeJS-WHATWG reference column threw ERR for U7 (Options A/B are what OTHER parsers produced, kept in paper_claimed_behavior).

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Pitfall 5 (Automatic Punycode Conversion), Table 3 U8 (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
notes: İ (U+0130) then @. rurl takes the clean UserInfo interpretation: @ delimits -> userinfo n.prİ, host e.gg (dropped userinfo per warning-userinfo policy). The paper's parsers variously fold the dotted-İ into the host (Options A-D); rurl's parse is defensible but diverges from the WHATWG-ref (A).

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Section 6.2 (GSB test vector) (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
paper_claimed_behavior: Section 6.2: GSB's always-flagged-malware test vector (the benign/known-bad baseline for the evasion PoCs)
notes: Google Safe Browsing's canonical always-flagged test URL. .test is a reserved non-resolvable TLD -> warning-invalid-tld (correct).

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Section 6.2 (GSB API/web evasion via Pitfall 3) (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
paper_claimed_behavior: Section 6.2: GSB API+web over-decode %2F->/ -> see letsencrypt.org as host -> report CLEAN, while browsers fetch malware.testing.google.test (false negative)
notes: %2F stays encoded in the UserInfo for a conformant parser -> @ delimits -> host malware.testing.google.test (the fetched/dangerous host). rurl matches the browser side; the attack fools GSB, not the parser.

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Section 6.2 (GSB API evasion via backslash correction) (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
paper_claimed_behavior: Section 6.2: GSB API decodes %5C->\ then backslash-corrects -> see letsencrypt.org -> report CLEAN, while browsers fetch malware.testing.google.test
notes: %5C stays encoded in the UserInfo -> host malware.testing.google.test. rurl matches the browser side.

source_reference: Reynolds et al., 'Equivocal URLs', ESORICS 2022, Section 6.2 (GSB web-interface evasion via backslash correction) (no artifact released; hand-transcribed from the paper PDF text layer)
paper_claimed_behavior: Section 6.2: GSB WEB interface (no backslash correction) evaluates letsencrypt.org -> CLEAN, while a browser (backslash correction) fetches malware.testing.google.test
notes: Literal backslashes. WHATWG maps \ to / -> host malware.testing.google.test (path continues); RFC keeps them in userinfo -> host letsencrypt.org. rurl reproduces BOTH: whatwg=malware.testing.google.test, rfc=letsencrypt.org.
